revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

revision.comment: Programmatically generating so that if this gets imported to wikipedia as a draft, the bot will attribute it to Immanuelle and send them g6 warnings

{{神社| 名称=熊本県護国神社| 所在地=熊本県熊本市宮内3-1| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|32|48|27|N|130|41|58|E|scale:30000}}| 祭神=熊本県出身の護国の[[英霊]]| 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社| 創建=[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）| 例祭=[[4月1日]]
[[10月10日]]}} '''熊本県護国神社'''（くまもとけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 [[明治維新]]から[[大東亜戦争]]までの国難に殉じた熊本県出身または縁故のある護国の[[英霊]]約6万5千柱を祀る。 ==歴史== [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、昭和14年、招魂社の制度改正により内務大臣指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、昭和32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 昭和39年に熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を、昭和40年に軍需工場で被爆した学徒殉死者の霊を合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 ==外部リンク== *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.com/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） [[Category:熊本県の神社|くまもとけんここくしんしや]] [[Category:護国神社|くまもとけんここくしんしや]] [[Category:別表神社|くまもとけんここくしんしや]] {{shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: ページ [[熊本縣護國神社]] を [[熊本県護国神社]] へ移動
revision.text:

[[10月10日]] |神事 = }} '''熊本県護国神社'''（くまもとけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 == 祭神 == [[明治維新]]から[[大東亜戦争]]（[[太平洋戦争]]）までの国難に殉じた熊本県出身または縁故のある護国の[[英霊]]約6万5千柱を祀る。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）、招魂社の制度改正により[[内務大臣]]指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、同32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を昭和39年に、軍需工場で被爆した学徒戦没者の霊を翌40年に合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 ==外部リンク== *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.com/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） {{DEFAULTSORT:くまもとけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:熊本県の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:熊本県の旧県社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:熊本市]] {{shinto-stub}}

[[10月10日]] |神事 = }} [[file:Kumamoto-pref_Gokokujinjya-shrine_2.jpg|thumb|right|境内]] '''熊本県護国神社'''（くまもとけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 == 祭神 == [[明治維新]]から[[大東亜戦争]]（[[太平洋戦争]]）までの国難に殉じた熊本県出身または縁故のある護国の[[英霊]]約6万5千柱を祀る。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）、招魂社の制度改正により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、同32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を昭和39年に、軍需工場で被爆した学徒戦没者の霊を翌40年に合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 == 参考文献 == * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、133頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、430-431頁 == 外部リンク == *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.com/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） {{DEFAULTSORT:くまもとけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:熊本県の旧県社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:中央区 (熊本市)]] {{shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 熊本県護国神社 |画像 = [[画像:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 1.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = [[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]宮内3-1 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 熊本県出身の護国の英霊 |社格 = 旧内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社 |創建 = 明治2年（1869年） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月1日]]
[[10月10日]] |神事 = }} [[file:Kumamoto-pref_Gokokujinjya-shrine_2.jpg|thumb|right|境内]] '''熊本県護国神社'''（くまもとけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 == 祭神 == [[明治維新]]から[[大東亜戦争]]（[[太平洋戦争]]）までの国難に殉じた熊本県出身または縁故のある護国の[[英霊]]約6万5千柱を祀る。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）、招魂社の制度改正により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、同32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を昭和39年に、軍需工場で被爆した学徒戦没者の霊を翌40年に合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 == 関連図書 == * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、133頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、430-431頁 == 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|熊本県護国神社}} *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまもとけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]]

{{神社 |名称 = 熊本県護国神社 |画像 = [[画像:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 1.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = [[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]宮内3-1 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 熊本県出身の護国の英霊 |社格 = 旧内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社 |創建 = 明治2年（1869年） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月1日]]

{{神社 |名称 = 熊本県護国神社 |画像 = [[画像:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 1.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = [[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]宮内3-1 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=熊本県護国神社}} |祭神 = 熊本県出身の護国の英霊 |社格 = 旧内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社 |創建 = 明治2年（1869年） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月1日]]
[[10月10日]] |神事 = }} [[file:Kumamoto-pref_Gokokujinjya-shrine_2.jpg|thumb|right|境内]] '''熊本県護国神社'''（くまもとけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 == 祭神 == [[明治維新]]から[[大東亜戦争]]（[[太平洋戦争]]）までの国難に殉じた熊本県出身または縁故のある護国の[[英霊]]約6万5千柱を祀る。 また、境内には 女性の身でありながら危険な前線で、敵味方を問わず傷病兵たちを献身的に看護して敵襲により犠牲となった [[従軍看護婦]]たちの慰霊碑が建立されている。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）、招魂社の制度改正により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、同32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を昭和39年に、軍需工場で被爆した学徒戦没者の霊を翌40年に合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 == 関連図書 == * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、133頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、430-431頁 == 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまもとけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]]

また、境内には 女性の身でありながら危険な前線で、敵味方を問わず傷病兵たちを献身的に看護して敵襲により犠牲となった [[従軍看護婦]]たちの慰霊碑が建立されている。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）、招魂社の制度改正により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、同32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を昭和39年に、軍需工場で被爆した学徒戦没者の霊を翌40年に合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 == 関連図書 == * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、133頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、430-431頁 == 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまもとけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]]

It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> == Referenc...'

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:09 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 熊本県護国神社 |画像 = [[画像:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 1.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = [[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]宮内3-1 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=熊本県護国神社}} |祭神 = 熊本県出身の戦没者 |社格 = 旧内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社 |創建 = 明治2年（1869年） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月1日]]
[[10月10日]] |神事 = }} [[file:Kumamoto-pref_Gokokujinjya-shrine_2.jpg|thumb|right|境内]] '''熊本県護国神社'''（くまもとけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 == 祭神 == [[明治維新]]から[[太平洋戦争]]（[[大東亜戦争]]）までの国難に殉じた熊本県出身または縁故のある戦没者約6万5千柱を祀る。
また、境内には 女性の身でありながら危険な前線で、敵味方を問わず傷病兵たちを献身的に看護したにもかかわらず、敵襲により犠牲となった [[従軍看護婦]]たちの慰霊碑が建立されている。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）、招魂社の制度改正により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、同32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を昭和39年に、軍需工場で被爆した学徒戦没者の霊を翌40年に合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 == 関連図書 == * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、133頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、430-431頁 == 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまもとけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]] [[category:熊本市中央区の建築物]]

{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 1.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]{{Efn|{{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} and {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and the Ministry of Home Affairs was abolished, so they were not designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine.}}, or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 1.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]<ref>https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> [[ja:熊本県護国神社]]
{{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine".
Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}}</ref><!-- derived from content I added to [[Gokoku shrines]] from an english source -->

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:09 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 熊本県護国神社 |画像 = [[画像:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = [[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]宮内3-1 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=熊本県護国神社}} |祭神 = 熊本県出身の戦没者 |社格 = 旧内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社 |創建 = 明治2年（1869年） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月1日]]
[[10月10日]] |神事 = }} [[file:Kumamoto-pref_Gokokujinjya-shrine_2.jpg|thumb|right|境内]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|みたま祭の提灯が掛かる境内]] '''熊本県護国神社'''（くまもとけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 == 祭神 == [[明治維新]]から[[太平洋戦争]]（[[大東亜戦争]]）までの国難に殉じた熊本県出身または縁故のある戦没者約6万5千柱を祀る。
また、境内には 女性の身でありながら危険な前線で、敵味方を問わず傷病兵たちを献身的に看護したにもかかわらず、敵襲により犠牲となった [[従軍看護婦]]たちの慰霊碑が建立されている。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）、招魂社の制度改正により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、同32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を昭和39年に、軍需工場で被爆した学徒戦没者の霊を翌40年に合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 == 関連図書 == * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、133頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、430-431頁 == 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまもとけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]] [[category:熊本市中央区の建築物]]

{{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]<ref>https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> [[ja:熊本県護国神社]]
{{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine".
Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}}</ref><!-- derived from content I added to [[Gokoku shrines]] from an english source -->
== See Also ==
* [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]]
== References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

護国神社|coordinates= {{coord2|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58|E}}}} }} [[file:Kumamoto-pref_Gokokujinjya-shrine_2.jpg|thumb|right|境内]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|みたま祭の提灯が掛かる境内]] '''熊本県護国神社'''（くまもとけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 == 祭神 == [[明治維新]]から[[太平洋戦争]]（[[大東亜戦争]]）までの国難に殉じた熊本県出身または縁故のある戦没者約6万5千柱を祀る。
また、境内には 女性の身でありながら危険な前線で、敵味方を問わず傷病兵たちを献身的に看護したにもかかわらず、敵襲により犠牲となった [[従軍看護婦]]たちの慰霊碑が建立されている。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）、招魂社の制度改正により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、同32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を昭和39年に、軍需工場で被爆した学徒戦没者の霊を翌40年に合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 == 関連図書 == * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、133頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、430-431頁 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまもとけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]] [[category:熊本市中央区の建築物]]

{{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社[[ja:熊本県護国神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]<ref>https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> [[ja:熊本県護国神社]]
{{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine".
Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}}</ref><!-- derived from content I added to [[Gokoku shrines]] from an english source -->
== See Also ==
* [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]]

It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} ==ja== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama Festival]] '''Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (熊本県護国神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Original pages]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Original pages]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.text:
{{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社[[ja:熊本県護国神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} ==ja== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama Festival]] '''Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (熊本県護国神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Original pages]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama Festival]] '''Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (熊本県護国神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社[[ja:熊本県護国神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} ==ja== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]

revision.comment: adding religious affiliation to the ones with a broken religious affiliation section
revision.text:
{{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社[[ja:熊本県護国神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} ==ja== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] '''Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (熊本県護国神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:original pages]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} ==ja== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] '''Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (熊本県護国神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社[[ja:熊本県護国神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} ==ja== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]

Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社[[ja:熊本県護国神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} ==ja== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] '''Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (熊本県護国神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} ==ja== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] '''Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (熊本県護国神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社[[Category:dd]][[ja:熊本県護国神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[Category:dd]][[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[Category:dd]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} *
