Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:From Everybodywiki with needed marking of original title]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} alongside {{Interlanguage link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:qq\]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}
