needed|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō-ku, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]]

revision.comment: 37 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:熊本県護国神社 up to rev 102021414
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:09 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 熊本県護国神社 |画像 = [[画像:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = [[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]][[宮内 (熊本市)|宮内]]3-1 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=熊本県護国神社}} |祭神 = 熊本県出身の戦没者 |社格 = 旧内務大臣指定[[護国神社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建 = [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月1日]]
[[10月10日]] |神事 = |地図={{Location map|Japan Kumamoto|label=熊本県
護国神社|coordinates= {{coord2|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58|E}}}} }} [[file:Kumamoto-pref_Gokokujinjya-shrine_2.jpg|thumb|right|境内]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|みたま祭の提灯が掛かる境内]] '''熊本県護国神社'''（くまもとけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[中央区 (熊本市)|中央区]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 == 祭神 == [[明治維新]]から[[太平洋戦争]]（[[大東亜戦争]]）までの国難に殉じた熊本県出身または縁故のある戦没者約6万5千柱を祀る。
また、境内には 女性の身でありながら危険な前線で、敵味方を問わず傷病兵たちを献身的に看護したにもかかわらず、敵襲により犠牲となった [[従軍看護婦]]たちの慰霊碑が建立されている。 == 歴史 == [[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）、[[熊本藩]]主[[細川韶邦]]と[[細川護久]]が、明治維新に殉じた藩士150柱を祀るために市内花岡山に[[招魂社]]を建立したのに始まる。明治7年に官祭の招魂社となり、[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）、招魂社の制度改正により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定の熊本県護国神社となった。 昭和19年、市内黒髪町の立田山麓に社地を得て、新しい社殿の造営を開始したが、終戦により中止された。昭和28年、現在地である、かつて招魂祭が行われていた藤崎台招魂場跡にて社殿の造営を再開し、同32年に竣工・遷座した。花岡山のかつての社地は飛地境内「花岡山招魂社」となった。 熊本県関係の公務殉職者（自衛官・警察官・消防士）の霊を昭和39年に、軍需工場で被爆した学徒戦没者の霊を翌40年に合祀した。昭和34年、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列された。 == 関連図書 == * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、133頁 * 角川日本地名大辞典編纂委員会編『角川日本地名大辞典43 熊本県』[[角川書店]]、1987年、430-431頁 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ 熊本県護国神社]（公式サイト） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまもとけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]] [[category:熊本市中央区の建築物]]

Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|12=draft}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=draft}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|12=draft}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=draft}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=draft}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{citation needed|Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=draft}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|qq=draft}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=draft}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{citation needed|Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{citation needed|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]]

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=draft}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|qq=draft}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=draft}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines.{{citation needed|Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{citation needed|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]]

The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]]

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō-ku|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto (city)|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=draft}} | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|qq=draft}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|ja|中央区 (熊本市)|lt=Chūō Ward|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto City|ja|熊本市|lt=Kumamoto City|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|ja|熊本県|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|qq=draft}}, Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]]

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]]

Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]]

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:熊本市の神社]]

View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:熊本市の神社]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の建築物]]

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[zh:熊本县护国神社]]

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[zh:熊本县护国神社]]
