Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の建築物]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の歴史]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[zh:熊本县护国神社]]

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の建築物]] [[Category:History of Chūō Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[zh:熊本县护国神社]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:熊本市中央区の建築物]] [[Category:History of Chūō Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[zh:熊本县护国神社]]

Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11568337]] Wikidata name: Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings in Chuo Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:History of Chūō Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[zh:熊本县护国神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11568337}}

revision.text:
[[sn:commons:Category:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:熊本県護国神社]][[zh:熊本县护国神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568337]] Wikidata name: Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|熊本県護国神社|version=102021414|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] {{everybodywiki link|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:熊本県護国神社]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kumamoto]] [[Category:Buildings in Chuo Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:History of Chūō Ward, Kumamoto City]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Commons category|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[zh:熊本县护国神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11568337}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} [[sn:commons:Category:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:熊本県護国神社]][[zh:熊本县护国神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568337]] Wikidata name: Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | locale = [[Kumamoto]] | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]'' | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja (Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Kumamoto Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987, pp.430-431. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [http://www.kumamoto-gokoku.jp/ Kumamoto-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Official Website) {{shinto-stub}} Torii and Haiden (worship hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Kumamoto | coordinates = {{Coord|32|48|27.8|N|130|41|58.0|E|region:JP-43_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 3-1 Miyauchi, [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō-ku]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Kumamoto Prefecture | type = Former Home Ministry-designated Gokoku Jinja, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = Meiji 2 (1869) | honden = Nagare-zukuri style | festival = April 1, October 10 }} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (熊本県護国神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chūō-ku, Kumamoto|Chūō Ward]], [[:en:Kumamoto|Kumamoto City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], Japan.https://shiromegurin.com/en/facility/gosyuin/spot/. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} [[File:Kumamoto-pref Gokokujinjya-shrine 2.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Kumamotoken-Gokoku-jinja, Lanterns of the Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|Lanterns hung during the Mitama festival]] == Deities Enshrined == The shrine enshrines approximately 65,000 spirits of those with ties to Kumamoto Prefecture who died in service to the nation, from the [[Meiji Restoration]] through the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]] (also known as the Greater East Asia War).
Within the precincts, there is also a memorial monument to female military nurses who served on dangerous front lines, caring for wounded soldiers of both friend and foe, and who lost their lives during enemy attacks. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), Kumamoto Domain lords Hosokawa Yorikuni and Hosokawa Morihisa established a Shōkonsha (invitation-of-spirits shrine) on Hanaokayama hill in the city to enshrine 150 domain samurai who died for the Meiji Restoration. In Meiji 7 (1874), it became a state-recognized Shōkonsha, and in Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as the Kumamoto Prefectural Gokoku Jinja. In Shōwa 19 (1944), the shrine acquired new land at the foot of Mt. Tateda in Kurokami-cho for new construction, but the work was halted due to the end of the war. In Shōwa 28 (1953), construction resumed at the current site, the former Fujisakidai Shōkonjō (a site used for shōkon rituals), and in Shōwa 32 (1957), the shrine was completed and relocated there. The old Hanaokayama site became a detached precinct known as Hanaokayama Shōkonsha. In Shōwa 39 (1964), the spirits of Kumamoto-related individuals who died in public service (Self-Defense Forces personnel, police officers, firefighters) were enshrined, and in Shōwa 40 (1965), the spirits of student soldiers who died in munitions factories were also enshrined. In Shōwa 34 (1959), it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Related Literature == * Eiji Shirai, Masakuni Doki (editors): "Jinja Jiten" (Encyclopedia of Shrines), Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p.133 * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee: "Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43 Kumamoto-ken," Kadokawa Shoten, 1987,
